chunks.text: May 4, 2020: Daniel Fremont wins ACM SIGBED Dissertation Award. Freshly-graduate CS Ph.D. student Daniel J. Fremont (advisor: Sanjit Seshia) has won the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) Special Interest Group on Embedded Systems (SIGBED) Paul Caspi Memorial Dissertation Award for his thesis on 'Algorithmic Improvisation.' The award, established in 2013, recognizes outstanding doctoral dissertations that significantly advance the state of the field.

April 16, 2020: Enabling robots to learn from past experiences. EECS Prof. Pieter Abbeel and Assistant Prof. Sergey Levine are developing algorithms that enable robots to learn from past experiences — and even from other robots. They use deep reinforcement learning to bring robots past a crucial threshold in demonstrating human-like intelligence: the ability to independently solve problems and master new tasks.

April 16, 2020: Using machine-learning to reinvent cybersecurity two ways: Song and Popa. EECS Prof. and alumna Dawn Song (Ph.D. '02, advisor: Doug Tygar) and Assistant Prof. Raluca Ada Popa are featured in the cover story for the Spring 2020 issue of the Berkeley Engineer titled 'Reinventing Cybersecurity.' They address protecting users' personal data while enabling data sharing for beneficial purposes.

April 10, 2020: Pieter Abbeel and Sergey Levine: teaching computers to teach themselves. EECS Prof. Pieter Abbeel and Assistant Prof. Sergey Levine appear in a New York Times article titled 'Computers Already Learn From Us. But Can They Teach Themselves?' The article explores approaches to help machines develop common sense and self-learning capabilities.

February 24, 2020: Researchers develop novel way to shrink light to detect ultra-tiny substances. EE Associate Prof. Boubacar Kanté and his graduate student Junhee Park are profiled in a Berkeley Engineering article. They created light-based technology to detect biological substances with high sensitivity, potentially revolutionizing diagnostics.

February 23, 2020: Keeping classified information secret in a world of quantum computing. Computer Science and Global Studies double major Jake Tibbetts published an article in the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists. He discusses strategies to protect classified information against threats from quantum computing advancements.

February 19, 2020: New nonvolatile memory cells shrink circuits and speed searches. The work of Prof. Sayeef Salahuddin and grad student Ava Tan is featured in IEEE Spectrum. They developed content-addressable memory cells that enhance search speeds and enable more efficient computing systems.

November 12, 2019: Two EECS papers win 2019 ACM SIGPLAN Distinguished Paper Awards. Two papers co-authored by Berkeley EECS authors won awards at OOPSLA 2019. One paper is 'Duet: An Expressive Higher-Order Language and Linear Type System for Statically Enforcing Differential Privacy' by Prof. Dawn Song and others.

October 23, 2019: 'Oracle-Guided Component-Based Program Synthesis' wins 2020 ICSE Most Influential Paper Award. The paper co-authored by alumnus Susmit Jha, Sumit Gulwani, EECS Prof. Sanjit A. Seshia, and Ashish Tiwari will receive the award from ACM/IEEE International Conference on Software Engineering. It is based on Jha's Ph.D. dissertation.

October 23, 2019: Using deep learning to expertly detect hemorrhages in brain scans. A computer algorithm co-developed by Vision Group alumnus Weicheng Kuo, post doc Christian Hӓne, their advisor Prof. Jitendra Malik, and UCSF researchers outperformed expert radiologists in detecting tiny brain hemorrhages. This advance may improve medical diagnostics and patient treatment.
